figures.caption: Figure 2: Effect of the main components of our anisotropic diffusion framework on an image with resolution 128 × 128. From top to bottom, the rows illustrate: (1) the impact of the diffusivity coefficient α1, where the third column uses a geometric transition of the form α1(t) = αmin 1 ( αmax 1 /αmin 1 )t/T with αmin 1 = 0.5 and αmax 1 = 1k, and where the effect of the diffusion coefficient σ was disabled by setting α2 = 0; (2) the impact of the intensity coefficient α2, where the effect of the drift b was disabled by setting α1 = 0; (3) the impact of the anisotropy coefficient λ1, where the effect of the diffusion coefficient σ was disabled by setting α2 = 0; and (4) the impact of the anisotropy coefficient λ2, where the effect of the drift b was disabled by setting α1 = 0.

Figure 1: Our anisotropic diffusion framework preserves geometric features over longer time scales, thereby affecting reconstructability during generative sampling. Left: Visualization of our forward processes, decomposed into drift (deterministic destruction) and noise (stochastic destruction) components, at selected time points from t = 0 up to the terminal time t = T . From top to bottom: (1) isotropic noise without drift (as in traditional diffusion models), (2) anisotropic (edge-preserving) noise without drift, (3) isotropic drift (heat-equation–like blur) without noise, (4) anisotropic drift (edge-preserving blur) without noise, and (5) anisotropic drift combined with anisotropic noise. As t → T , a transition to isotropy is required since the backward generative process must start from fully destructed data, as discussed in Section 4.3. This transition is intentionally omitted here to isolate the effect of the individual components. Right: We demonstrate the geometry-awareness of the proposed approach on the stroke-to-image generation task SDEDIT (Meng et al., 2021). The isotropic, SDE-based model of Song et al. (2021b) preserves the geometric structure of the input stroke image substantially less faithfully than our anisotropic, SPDE-based model.

Figure 7: Stroke-to-image generation with SDEDIT (Section 5.6). Left block: samples from LSUN/CHURCH_OUTDOOR; right block: samples from LSUN/BEDROOM. Within each block, columns show (from left to right) the input stroke painting, SDEDIT results with Song et al. (2021b), and SDEDIT with Ours (anisotropic).

Figure 4: Evaluation metrics on CIFAR10 between 10k and 100k training iterations with Ours (anisotropic) in a fine-tuning experiment, where training was initialized from the checkpoint of Song et al. (2021b). For reference, applying our sampling procedure directly to that checkpoint — without further training — results in an IS of 1, FID of 678.3, and KID of 0.9. After 100k training iterations, these values improve substantially to an IS of 10.0, FID of 2.0, and KID of 6.4e-4

Figure 5: Generated samples on LSUN/BEDROOM from our pixel space experiment (Section 5.4). Left: Samples generated by Ours (anisotropic) (see Section 5.2), using a model trained from scratch — i.e., without initialization from a pre-trained checkpoint. Right: Samples generated by Song et al. (2021b), using a model trained from scratch as well. Ours (anisotropic) more faithfully resembles the geometric structure of the dataset.

Figure 6: Generated samples on LSUN/CHURCH_OUTDOOR from our latent space experiment (Section 5.5). Left: Samples generated by Ours (anisotropic) (see Section 5.2), using a model trained from scratch. Right: Samples generated by Song et al. (2021b), using a model trained from scratch as well. Ours (anisotropic) more faithfully resembles the geometric structure of the dataset.
